goal: Suggest preprocessing and feature-engineering actions for predicting short-horizon closing price movements from order-book and auction microstructure data under MAE.

task_characteristics.analysis_notes: Single-table time-series finance regression task. The train CSV contains approximately 5.24 million intraday rows keyed by (stock_id, date_id, seconds_in_bucket), placing it well into the xlarge bucket. Raw feature dimensionality is low at 16 columns (stock_id, date_id, seconds_in_bucket, imbalance_size, imbalance_buy_sell_flag, reference_price, matched_size, far_price, near_price, ask_price, bid_price, ask_size, bid_size, wap, target, row_id). Preprocessing complexity is medium at 20 golden actions because competitive pipelines build extensive microstructure imbalance formulas, pairwise price features, stock-level lag/diff/rolling temporal features, stock priors from grouped aggregations, and multiple missing-value handling strategies.

dataset_description.summary: A single train.csv file with approximately 5.24 million intraday order-book rows across 200 stocks and 481 trading days. Each row captures the auction state at a specific (stock_id, date_id, seconds_in_bucket) triple and includes quoted prices (reference, far, near, ask, bid, WAP), order sizes (imbalance, matched, ask, bid), and a directional imbalance flag. The target column is the 60-second closing price return. The example_test_files directory contains a sample test CSV and submission template.
dataset_description.raw_text: Optiver Trading at the Close is a Kaggle code competition focused on predicting short-horizon closing auction price movements using order-book microstructure data. The primary dataset is a single large CSV with repeated intraday rows per stock per trading day. Each row is identified by stock_id (integer 0-199), date_id (integer trading day), and seconds_in_bucket (seconds into the closing auction window). The feature set is compact but dense: six price columns (reference_price, far_price, near_price, ask_price, bid_price, wap), four size columns (imbalance_size, matched_size, ask_size, bid_size), and one directional flag (imbalance_buy_sell_flag). Missing values are concentrated in far_price and near_price (approximately 50% null), which represent indicative auction prices that are only published when there is sufficient order flow. The target is a normalized 60-second WAP return measured from the auction. The competition uses a code-submission format with a hidden test set served through a runtime API, but example test files are provided for local development.
